answer:
Drawing upon the radiant wisdom of the Prophetic traditions, which serve as an enduring framework for the soul and a clear light of guidance, I present the *Sahih* narrations from Bukhari and Muslim that anchor the necessity of *Ikhlas* (sincerity) in the testimony of faith. These reports clarify that the *Shahada* is not a hollow utterance but a movement of the heart that must be entirely directed toward seeking the pleasure of the Creator, as steadfast as the pillars of the Kaaba [1, 2].

### **The Most Fortunate of Mankind**

The Messenger of Allah provided a lucid presentation of the rewards of sincerity in his dialogue with Abu Huraira. When asked who would be the luckiest person to receive his intercession on the Day of Resurrection, he replied that it is the one who declares "None has the right to be worshipped but Allah" with absolute sincerity from the bottom of their heart [1]. This narration establishes that internal veracity is the primary criterion for spiritual success and the catalyst for Divine mercy [1].

### **The Safeguard of the Soul: The Narration of Mu’adh**

To ensure a seamless and accessible understanding of this truth, the Prophet provided a definitive guarantee to his companion Mu’adh bin Jabal. He observed that anyone who testifies sincerely from his heart that there is no god but Allah and that Muhammad is His bondsman and His messenger will be immuned from the Fire [2, 3]. This teaching serves as a robust foundation for the believer, emphasizing that the validity of the testimony is inextricably tied to the internal state of *Ikhlas* [2, 3].

### **Seeking the Face of God**

The scriptures define true sincerity as a state where the believer acts "seeking thereby the Face of God" [4, 5]. The Prophet explicitly stated that Allah has forbidden the Fire for the person who says "There is no god but Allah" while sincerely seeking only Allah's pleasure [4, 5]. This requirement ensures that the believer’s journey is characterized by beauty and grace, free from the contamination of showy piety or worldly motives [5, 6].

**A Synthesis of Sincerity**
As scholarly insights within the sources explain, the spending of one's breath and wealth for the sake of Allah purifies the heart and affirms the truth that nothing is dearer to the soul than its Master [6]. This sincerity is the "most trustworthy handhold" that enables the believer to withstand any trial, turning the instruction of the *Shahada* into a living, vibrant reality [7, 8].

have seen one (crystallized into reality), and I am waiting for the other. He told us: Trustworthiness descended in the innermost (root) of the hearts of people. Then the Qur'an was revealed and they learnt from the Qur'an and they learnt from the Sunnah. Then he (the Holy Prophet) told us about the removal of trustworthiness. He said: The man would have a wink of sleep and trustworthiness would be taken away from his heart leaving the impression of a faint mark. He would again sleep and trustworthiness would be taken away from his heart leaving an impression of a blister, as if you rolled down an ember on your foot and it was vesicled. He would see a swelling having nothing in it. He (the Holy Prophet) then took up a pebble and rolled it down over his foot and (said): The people would enter into transactions amongst one another and hardly a person would be left who would return (things) entrusted to him. (And there would be so much paucity of honest persons) till  it would be said: There in such a such tribe is a trustworthy man. And they would also say about a per-son: How prudent he is, how broad-minded he is and how intelligent he is, whereas in his heart the-re would not be faith even to the weight of a mustard seed. I have passed through a time in which I did not care with whom amongst you I entered into a transaction, for if he were a Muslim his faith would compel him to discharge his obligations to me and it he were a Christian or a Jew, the ruler would compel him to discharge his obligations to me. But today I would not enter into a transaction with you except so and so.

Book 001, Number 0051: It is reported on the authority of Anas b. Malik that the Prophet of Allah (may peace and blessings

(KITAB AL-ZAKAT)

have seen one (crystallized into reality), and I am waiting for the other. He told us: Trustworthiness descended in the innermost (root) of the hearts of people. Then the Qur'an was revealed and they learnt from the Qur'an and they learnt from the Sunnah. Then he (the Holy Prophet) told us about the removal of trustworthiness. He said: The man would have a wink of sleep and trustworthiness would be taken away from his heart leaving the impression of a faint mark. He would again sleep and trustworthiness would be taken away from his heart leaving an impression of a blister, as if you rolled down an ember on your foot and it was vesicled. He would see a swelling having nothing in it. He (the Holy Prophet) then took up a pebble and rolled it down over his foot and (said): The people would enter into transactions amongst one another and hardly a person would be left who would return (things) entrusted to him. (And there would be so much paucity of honest persons) till  it would be said: There in such a such tribe is a trustworthy man. And they would also say about a per-son: How prudent he is, how broad-minded he is and how intelligent he is, whereas in his heart the-re would not be faith even to the weight of a mustard seed. I have passed through a time in which I did not care with whom amongst you I entered into a transaction, for if he were a Muslim his faith would compel him to discharge his obligations to me and it he were a Christian or a Jew, the ruler would compel him to discharge his obligations to me. But today I would not enter into a transaction with you except so and so.

It should also be remembered that zakat or sadaqa in Islam is not a voluntary act of charity which a rich man gives to the poor out of his own sweet will, but it is an obligatory act which every Muslim is enjoined upon to perform if he is sincere in his belief in God and the Hereafter. Thus there is no burden of obligation on one who receives zakat, but a sense of thankfulness and gratitude on the part of the giver, since he has been enabled by the recipient to discharge his obligation that he owes to God and society.
